behaviors.description: Traveling-salesman heuristic produces routes ≤1.5x optimal for ≤20 stops
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithmic test requiring known coordinates and distance calculations, but no I/O. To catch a real failure (heuristic producing poor-quality routes), you test the algorithm directly with mocked coordinates and verified distance calculations. The mock is in-process and doesn't hide the failure if the algorithm itself is wrong.
behaviors.test_description: Run the TSP algorithm on 5–10 test cases with known optimal solutions (precomputed for small instances or from benchmarks). Use fixed coordinate sets; mock the geocoding API. For each case, compute the route length and verify it is ≤1.5× the optimal. This catches failures where the heuristic produces suboptimal routes due to incorrect distance calculations, route ordering, or selection logic.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test directly exercises the algorithm in isolation, which aligns with Unit-level testing—no I/O, no real dependencies.

behaviors.description: Time window estimates computed correctly from distance and road-type speed
behaviors.justification: This is a pure calculation: estimated_time = distance ÷ speed. No I/O, no external dependencies. To catch a real failure (wrong time estimates), you mock distances and speeds, then verify the formula. A Unit test directly exercises the calculation logic.
behaviors.test_description: Test the time-estimation function with parametrized inputs: multiple (distance, road_type, expected_speed) tuples covering highway, city, residential, etc. For each tuple, verify that estimated_time = distance ÷ speed_for_road_type. Also test edge cases (zero distance, very slow speed). This catches failures like using distance × speed instead of ÷, or applying wrong speeds for road types.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Pure formula testing requires no integration; Unit level is exactly right.

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects over-length routes, returns correct excess
behaviors.justification: Pure validation logic: if total_time > shift_hours, reject and return (total_time − shift_hours). No I/O, no dependencies. To catch a real failure (accepting a route that overruns shift hours, or returning wrong excess), test the validator in isolation with various route times and shift limits.
behaviors.test_description: Test the shift-hours validator with parametrized cases: (total_time, shift_hours) pairs where some exceed the limit and others fit within it. Verify: (a) routes exceeding shift hours are rejected, (b) excess is calculated as (total_time − shift_hours), (c) routes within limits are accepted. Edge cases: exactly at limit, zero hours remaining, negative times (error case). This catches failures like off-by-one errors, incorrect subtraction, or wrong comparison operators.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level logic test; no dependencies or I/O needed.

behaviors.description: Service skips failed-geocoding addresses, optimizes remaining, reports skipped
behaviors.justification: To catch real failures in error handling—e.g., the service crashes or doesn't report skipped addresses when the geocoding API returns no results—you must test with a realistic simulation of the API failure mode. A pure Unit mock can be written badly and hide bugs; an Integration-level mock (e.g., a mock HTTP service or test fixture that simulates the API response format) is harder to get wrong and catches parsing, error-handling, and edge-case issues.
behaviors.test_description: Set up an integration test with a mocked geocoding service (HTTP mock or test fixture) that returns 'no results' for a known subset of addresses in the input list. Call the route optimization service with this list. Verify: (a) the optimized route does not include failed addresses, (b) a report lists which addresses were skipped, (c) the remaining addresses are optimized correctly, and (d) the service does not crash. This catches failures like uncaught exceptions on empty API results, missing error reporting, or incorrect filtering of failed addresses.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration-level test wires the service with a realistically mocked geocoding layer, catching issues that a naive Unit mock might miss.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses consolidated into single stop with combined items
behaviors.justification: Pure data-processing logic: detect duplicates, merge delivery items, remove duplicates from address list. No I/O, no dependencies. To catch a real failure (duplicates not consolidated, items lost, or address visited twice), test the consolidation logic directly with test data.
behaviors.test_description: Test the duplicate-consolidation function with input lists containing known duplicates (e.g., two or three copies of the same address, each with different delivery items). Verify: (a) the consolidated list contains each address once, (b) delivery items for duplicate addresses are correctly combined or counted, (c) the order of remaining (non-duplicate) addresses is preserved or deterministic. Edge cases: all addresses duplicated, no duplicates, items with conflicting metadata. This catches failures like incomplete merging, lost items, or duplicates remaining in the output.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level data transformation; no I/O or service integration needed.
